examples.title: Simple Amplitude Modulation
examples.description: Basic AM modulation algorithm demonstrating carrier modulation
examples.code:
ALGORITHM AM_MODULATION
INPUT: message_signal[m], carrier_frequency fc, sampling_rate fs
OUTPUT: modulated_signal[m]

BEGIN
 carrier_amplitude = 1.0
 modulation_index = 0.5 // 0 to 1
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO m-1 DO
 // Generate carrier wave
 carrier = carrier_amplitude * sin(2 * PI * fc * i / fs)
 
 // Modulate with message signal
 modulated_signal[i] = carrier * (1 + modulation_index * message_signal[i])
 END FOR
 
 RETURN modulated_signal
END

// Demodulation
ALGORITHM AM_DEMODULATION
INPUT: modulated_signal[m], carrier_frequency fc, sampling_rate fs
OUTPUT: recovered_message[m]

BEGIN
 // Envelope detection (simple absolute value + smoothing)
 FOR i FROM 0 TO m-1 DO
 envelope[i] = ABS(modulated_signal[i])
 END FOR
 
 // Low-pass filter to recover message
 recovered_message = LOW_PASS_FILTER(envelope, cutoff = fc * 0.1)
 
 RETURN recovered_message
END
examples.explanation: This demonstrates basic AM modulation where the message signal modifies the amplitude of a high-frequency carrier. The modulation index controls how much the message affects the carrier. Demodulation extracts the original message using envelope detection.

examples.title: Signal Strength Calculation
examples.description: Calculate received signal strength using free space path loss model
examples.explanation: Calculates received signal strength using free space path loss model. This follows inverse square law for signal power - every 6dB doubling distance reduces power to 1/4. Essential for wireless link budget calculations.

examples.title: Channel Capacity Calculation
examples.description: Shannon's theorem implementation for maximum data rate
examples.explanation: Implements Shannon's theorem showing fundamental limit of channel capacity. Spectral efficiency indicates how efficiently bandwidth is used. Higher SNR or wider bandwidth increases capacity.

examples.title: Simple FHSS Frequency Hopper
examples.description: Demonstrates frequency hopping spread spectrum concept
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FHSS_TRANSMIT
INPUT: data_stream[], hop_pattern[], num_channels, dwell_time
OUTPUT: transmitted_signals[]

BEGIN
 channel_index = 0
 current_channel = hop_pattern[0]
 
 FOR each data_chunk IN data_stream DO
 // Transmit on current frequency channel
 frequency = channel_frequencies[current_channel]
 transmit(data_chunk, frequency)
 
 // Wait for dwell time
 WAIT(dwell_time)
 
 // Move to next channel in hop pattern
 channel_index = (channel_index + 1) MOD length(hop_pattern)
 current_channel = hop_pattern[channel_index]
 END FOR
END

// Bluetooth hop pattern generation (simplified)
ALGORITHM GENERATE_HOP_PATTERN
INPUT: device_address, clock
OUTPUT: hop_sequence[79]

BEGIN
 // Bluetooth uses pseudo-random hop selection
 // 79 channels in 2.4 GHz band (2402-2480 MHz)
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO 78 DO
 hop_sequence[i] = (device_address + clock) MOD 79
 END FOR
 
 RETURN hop_sequence
END
examples.explanation: Demonstrates FHSS where transmission frequency changes according to a pseudo-random pattern. Used in Bluetooth for frequency diversity - avoids interference and provides security. Each hop typically dwells 625μs in Bluetooth.
